evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
5/14 H2s are questions (36% — target ≥40%)
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
definitional posts should have DefinedTerm schema — not present
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
15 em-dashes (4.57 per 400 words; target <1)
16 tricolons (6.09/500 words; target ≤2)
Passive-voice ratio 18% (target <15%)
Human voice vs committee 1/10 — Reads like a template produced by a marketing committee, with no individual voice anywhere.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 5/10 — A couple of opinionated jabs exist, but most claims hedge into generic best-practice territory.
Unexpected phrasing 1/10 — Entirely boilerplate phrasing; 'dominate product discovery,' 'informational landscape,' 'decision journeys.'
Specific citations (names, prices, dates) 3/10 — Names a few LLMs and one schema type, but zero prices, dates, people, or concrete case data.
Point of view vs neutral survey 2/10 — Balanced, encyclopedic framing throughout; takes no stance.
Quotability 1/10 — Nothing here is memorable or forwardable.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with duplicated nav/title clutter and a generic TL;DR. Nothing hooks the reader in the first two sentences.

intro_hook 5/10 — The first two body sentences are pure definition-throat-clearing. The most arresting fact in the entire post—the 34% vs 4% citation-share collapse—is buried four sections deep. The hook should lead with that concrete asymmetry to earn attention immediately. The TL;DR block above the byline partially salvages this, but it is pre-article UI, not the article's own hook.

specificity 6/10 — Several bullet-point summary lines ('It influences brand perception in generative search,' 'Emphasize freshness and authority in all content,' 'Improvements stem from iterative schema enhancements') float in abstraction with no named examples, numbers, or concrete scenarios. The post earns its specificity points from the 34%/4% stat, the named tools with pricing (Profound at $499/mo), and the schema.org/DefinedTerm call-out—but those islands are surrounded by generic filler bullets that an editor should cut or concretize.
